perturbation.original_text: 6.2 The treatment of all sensitive and personal data sh all be compliant to the relevant national legislation in place, particularly in relation to law….
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2 The treatment of all sensitive and personal data shall adhere to standard business practices.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement to comply with national data protection laws. This violates EU law if the company involved processes personal data of EU citizens.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the obligation to comply with GDPR, which mandates specific principles for processing personal data, including lawfulness, fairness, and transparency. By stating adherence to 'standard business practices', it allows for practices that may not be compliant with GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 5 GDPR – Principles relating to processing of personal data - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Personal data shall be: processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner in relation to the data subject (‘lawfulness, fairness and transparency’); collected for specified, explicit and legitimate purposes and not further processed in a manner that is incompatible with those purposes; further processing for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes shall, in accordance with Article 89 (1), not be considered to be incompatible with the initial purposes (‘purpose limitation’); adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed (‘data minimisation’); accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date; every reasonable step must be taken to ensure that personal data that are inaccurate, having regard to the purposes for which they are processed, are erased or rectified without delay (‘accuracy’); kept in a form which permits identification of data subjects for no longer than is necessary for the purposes for which the personal data are processed; personal data may be stored for longer periods insofar as the personal data will be processed solely for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89( 1) subject to implementation of the appropriate technical and organisational measures required by this Regulation in order to safeguard the rights and freedoms of the data subject (‘storage limitation’); processed in a manner that ensures appropriate security of the personal data, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing and against accidental loss, destruction or damage, using appropriate technical or organisational measures (‘integrity and confidentiality’).
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with national data protection laws, while the changed text only requires adherence to 'standard business practices'. This omission contradicts GDPR, which is a legally mandated consumer protection according to the definition provided. Scraped Law Snippet 2, Art. 5 GDPR, specifies that personal data must be 'processed lawfully, fairly and in a transparent manner'. Replacing legal compliance with 'standard business practices' allows for practices that may not meet these legal requirements, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.4 The information and data of a confidential nature will be forwarded by ACRONYM OF THE PINE PROJECT PARTNER to the financing institution of the PROJECT (EACI, the Executive Agency for Competitiveness and Innovation of the Eu ropean Commission), or to other bodies designated to monitoring or control, compliant to t he envisaged management procedures, in an anonymous or in an aggregated form and however without providing information that may allow the association of the provided data with the COMPANY .
perturbation.changed_text: 4.4 The information and data of a confidential nature will be forwarded by ACRONYM OF THE PINE PROJECT PARTNER to the financing institution of the PROJECT (EACI, the Executive Agency for Competitiveness and Innovation of the European Commission), or to other bodies designated to monitoring or control, as deemed necessary by the project managers.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the anonymity and aggregation requirement, potentially allowing direct association of data with the company, violating data protection principles, if EU citizens' data is involved.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of GDPR requires a lawful basis for processing personal data. By removing the requirement for anonymization and aggregation, the modified text allows for processing of personal data without ensuring a lawful basis, contradicting GDPR.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 6 GDPR – Lawfulness of processing - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies: the data subject has given consent to the processing of his or her personal data for one or more specific purposes; processing is necessary for the performance of a contract to which the data subject is party or in order to take steps at the request of the data subject prior to entering into a contract; processing is necessary for compliance with a legal obligation to which the controller is subject; processing is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; processing is necessary for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; processing is necessary for the purposes of the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party, except where such interests are overridden by the interests or fundamental rights and freedoms of the data subject which require protection of personal data, in particular where the data subject is a child. Point (f) of the first subparagraph shall not apply to processing carried out by public authorities in the performance of their tasks. Member States may maintain or introduce more specific provisions to adapt the application of the rules of this Regulation with regard to processing for compliance with points (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 by determining more precisely specific requirements for the processing and other measures to ensure lawful and fair processing including for other specific processing situations as provided for in Chapter IX . 1 The basis for the processing referred to in point (c) and (e) of paragraph 1 shall be laid down by: Union law; or Member State law to which the controller is subject. 2 The purpose of the processing shal
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the requirement for anonymization and aggregation of confidential data, which contradicts the principles of GDPR as it potentially allows for the processing of personal data without a lawful basis. The provided snippet of Art. 6 GDPR states that 'Processing shall be lawful only if and to the extent that at least one of the following applies...', which includes consent or necessity for contract performance or legal obligation. Removing the anonymization requirement makes it more likely that personal data is being processed without such a lawful basis, as the data can now be directly associated with the COMPANY. Therefore, the change weakens data protection and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Duration Confidentiality obligations are intended to last fr om the signing of the present AGREEMENT up to five years after completion of the PROJECT , currently envisaged for March 16, 2015, except any further extensions of the validity period of this AGREEMENT to be agreed upon by THE PARTIES and before the aforementioned deadline. In case THE PARTIES decide to extend the validity period for a longer period of time or unlimitedly, confide ntiality obligations are extended from the signing of the AGREEMENT for the entire duration as agreed upon, which shal l be inserted in the AGREMENT .
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Duration Confidentiality obligations are intended to last for the duration of the PROJECT, currently envisaged for March 16, 2015.
perturbation.explanation: This eliminates the five-year extension and the possibility of longer or unlimited confidentiality, potentially failing to adequately protect trade secrets as defined by law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: EU Directive 2016/943 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets)
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the EU Directive 2016/943 requires member states to provide measures, procedures and remedies necessary to ensure the protection of trade secrets. Limiting the confidentiality duration to the project duration may not be sufficient to protect trade secrets beyond the project's end, thus potentially violating the directive's objective.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the five-year extension of confidentiality obligations and the possibility of longer or unlimited confidentiality, which contradicts the EU Directive 2016/943. The directive aims to protect trade secrets, and limiting confidentiality to the project's duration may not be sufficient to safeguard trade secrets beyond the project's end. The original contract provides for a longer period of confidentiality by default, whereas the changed contract eliminates this extension, creating a scenario where trade secrets may not be adequately protected, violating the objective of the EU directive, as detailed in snippet 1: 'Directive (EU) 2016/943 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 8 June 2016 on the protection of undisclosed know-how and business information (trade secrets) against their unlawful acquisition, use and disclosure.'

perturbation.original_text: 9.2 In case of any controversy among THE PARTIES in relation to the execution and interpretation of the present AGREEMENT, which cannot be settled a micably, the competent court shall be the Court of …. …..
perturbation.changed_text: 9.2 In case of any controversy among THE PARTIES in relation to the execution and interpretation of the present AGREEMENT, all disputes will be resolved through binding arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: This forces arbitration, potentially waiving the COMPANY's right to a judicial trial, which may be prohibited in some jurisdictions or require explicit waiver. The location '… ….' is ambiguous, further complicating the issue.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Directive 93/13/EEC on unfair terms in consumer contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6(1) of Directive 93/13/EEC states that unfair terms shall not be binding on the consumer. If the COMPANY is considered a consumer (e.g., a small business), forcing binding arbitration without a clear and understandable explanation of the rights being waived could be deemed an unfair term, violating the directive. The replaced section, while incomplete, suggested a court jurisdiction, implying a potential right to judicial review, which is removed by the arbitration clause.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces the original clause regarding court jurisdiction with binding arbitration. The Law Explanation argues that forcing binding arbitration without a clear explanation of waived rights could be deemed an unfair term under Article 6(1) of Directive 93/13/EEC, potentially violating consumer rights if the COMPANY is considered a consumer. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows the directive exists: 'Council Directive 93/13/EEC of 5 April 1993 on unfair terms in consumer contracts'. This supports the argument that the change creates a contradiction because it removes a potential right to judicial review by imposing mandatory arbitration which, under the directive, may not be binding if considered unfair to a consumer. This constitutes an Omission Legal Contradiction, as the modification potentially removes a legally mandated consumer protection.
